sources.notes: Statement and constraints checked: p is a permutation, colors are in [1,n], total n is at most 2e5, and answers are modulo 998244353.
Official editorial and code checked for c'=c, the same-color exchange criterion, laminar feasible ranges, and DSU component-choice counting.
Used for the equivalent ascending-height deletion interpretation: deleting one item from the appropriate active equal-color run and multiplying its current size.
Cross-checked the same-color swap condition, nested feasible intervals, and the segment-tree placement-counting formulation.
Re-derived the active-run recurrence and compared its product against exhaustive enumeration of all candidate permutations on randomized instances up to n=8.

reference_candidates.notes: The Luogu solution index was checked but did not expose readable solution bodies in this session, so it was not used as evidence.
Linked accepted implementation for the deletion-style solution; direct source access was bot-protected, so the readable blog explanation and official code were used.

annotation.summary: Count permutations p' and color arrays c' that produce exactly the same colored block layout after every column falls under gravity.
annotation.constraints: Across test cases, sum(n) is at most 2e5; p is a permutation of 1..n and the result is required modulo 998244353.
annotation.core_idea: After gravity, column h contains, in preserved vertical order, the colors c_i of rows satisfying p_i>=h. Column 1 therefore fixes c'=c. Let pos[h] be the original position carrying height h. Moving from threshold h to h+1 deletes pos[h] from the active color sequence. To produce the same next sequence, height h may be assigned to exactly any position in the maximal active run having color c[pos[h]]; deleting any one of these indistinguishable colors gives the same remaining color sequence. Multiply the run size, canonically delete pos[h], splice it from a doubly linked active order, and union the newly adjacent runs when their colors agree. A DSU storing the number of still-active positions in each run supports all factors.
annotation.complexity: O(n*alpha(n)) time and O(n) memory per test case.
tricks: view every gravity-sorted column as a threshold-filtered color subsequence
column one proves that the color array is immutable
count permutations through a canonical ascending-height deletion sequence
rows inside one active equal-color run are indistinguishable for the next deletion
splice each position once and merge newly adjacent equal-color runs

solution_variants.name: active-color-run-deletion-dsu
solution_variants.summary: Initialize maximal adjacent equal-color runs in a DSU with active sizes. Process heights h=1..n using the inverse permutation. Multiply by the active size of pos[h]'s run, mark pos[h] deleted, splice it from predecessor/successor arrays, and union its live neighbors if their colors match.

solution_variants.name: per-color-laminar-range-dsu
solution_variants.summary: For each color, temporarily remove that color from a range-maximum segment tree, process its positions by increasing height, and union same-color positions reachable without crossing a different-color height at least as large. The reachable ranges are laminar; multiply component size minus already fixed positions.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n*log n) time and O(n) memory.

tags.evidence: DSU maintains the current equal-color choice components and their remaining active sizes as deletions expose new adjacencies.
There is a bijective recursive count in which height h has exactly the current equal-color run size choices, so the answer is the product of these factors.
Every position is spliced exactly once and every newly exposed adjacency causes at most one successful DSU merge.
In the official formulation, feasible same-color placement intervals are nested or disjoint, allowing components to grow monotonically while smaller heights are fixed.
The official variant uses range maxima over different-colored positions to detect the first height barrier preventing a same-color exchange.
The complete permutation is processed in known height order, turning layout equivalence into monotone component updates.
The official implementation sorts each color's positions by height, but the primary deletion formulation obtains the order directly from the inverse permutation.
